reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Excellent trigger-type coverage: all variants from alarms.d.ts are represented in tables with correct threshold properties, units, and defaults — source-grounded and exhaustive without being redundant.

The Info callout calling out the inverted comparison direction for database-free-storage and database-free-memory (LessThanThreshold) is a genuine gotcha that saves the reader debugging time. The JSDoc on those types confirms it.

Evaluation window section is exemplary decision-support: it defines the three properties, gives a worked numeric example, and the Tip callout lands an opinionated starting point for production error-rate alarms vs storage alarms.
CLI section correctly uses one-command-per-fenced-block with prose sentences introducing each variant, fully respecting the copy-button discipline.

FAQ is diverse and high-quality: mixes Stacktape-specific ('Which resources support alarms?'), AWS-service questions ('How much do CloudWatch alarms cost?'), and comparison questions ('When should I use alarms vs issues?'). Each answer is 2-4 self-contained sentences.
The 'When NOT to use' section is genuinely opinionated and correctly distinguishes alarms from logs, metrics, issues, and budgets — not filler.

Code examples use correct class names (LambdaFunction, RelationalDatabase, RdsEnginePostgres follow the valid naming conventions), are properly wrapped in CodeBlock intellisense, and use focus markers correctly.
problems: Missing <ApiReference> block entirely. The style guide states resource/concept pages should include exactly one <ApiReference definitionName="..." />. The alarms.d.ts file contains several candidate types (AlarmDefinitionBase, AlarmEvaluation, etc.) that belong in a reference section. A reader who exhausts the prose and wants to look up every property has no API reference to drill into.

The application-load-balancer-custom trigger section claims it 'lets you alarm on any of the 50+ CloudWatch ALB metrics' and names specific metrics (ActiveConnectionCount, TargetResponseTime, RequestCount, HTTPCode_Target_5XX_Count). The ApplicationLoadBalancerCustomTrigger type is listed in the union but its full definition — including the metric field name and what values it accepts — is not shown in the provided source. The '50+' count and those specific metric names are unsupported by the inline source files.

The disabledGlobalAlarms property and its code example are not visible in the provided source (alarms.d.ts or AlarmsRulesPage.tsx). The claim that its array 'takes the alarm names as configured in the Console' and the full example showing it on LambdaFunction is ungrounded in the provided source files.

Web service alarms section claims 'A web service accepts both HTTP API Gateway and Application Load Balancer alarm triggers.' The StpAlarmEnabledResource union in alarms.d.ts lists StpHttpApiGateway and StpApplicationLoadBalancer as separate entries — not StpWebService. The claim that a web service resource has a unified alarms property accepting both trigger families is not directly evidenced by the provided types.

The <ConsoleScreenshot> component used in the Global alarm rules section is not mentioned in the style guide, example page guidance, or any referenced MDX component palette. Its props (src, alt, caption) are unverified against the actual component definition. If this component doesn't exist or has different prop names, the page will fail to render.

For a first-time user audience, the five trigger-type subsections (each with a detailed table) arrive before a single 'getting started' code example. The reader has to scroll past several hundred words of reference tables before reaching a copyable Lambda alarm example. The happy path is buried.
mandatoryFixes: Add a single <ApiReference definitionName="AlarmDefinitionBase" /> (or the closest matching Props type from alarms.d.ts) at the bottom of the page. This is part of the default page rhythm for concept/resource pages and its absence leaves readers with no structured property reference.

Remove or narrow the claim about the application-load-balancer-custom trigger. Do not state '50+ CloudWatch ALB metrics' or name specific CloudWatch metric identifiers (ActiveConnectionCount, TargetResponseTime, etc.) unless the ApplicationLoadBalancerCustomTrigger type definition with its metric field is present in the source. Replace with a cautiously scoped statement grounded only in what the source shows.

Either remove the disabledGlobalAlarms code example and section prose entirely, or add a clear source citation. The property is not present in the provided alarms.d.ts or AlarmsRulesPage.tsx source files. If it is defined elsewhere (e.g. on LambdaFunctionProps), cite that; if not, remove the claim.

Verify and either ground or remove the web service alarms claim. The source StpAlarmEnabledResource union does not include StpWebService. Rewrite the section to say that web services surface alarms through their underlying networking resource (the ALB or HTTP API Gateway resource), making clear these are alarm types on the child resource, not a unified web service alarms property.

Verify <ConsoleScreenshot> is a valid, registered MDX component with the props used (src, alt, caption). If it is not, replace with a prose description of the Console alarms rules page (which is grounded in AlarmsRulesPage.tsx) without the screenshot component.
optionalImprovements: Move the Lambda function alarm example to immediately after the intro paragraph (before the trigger-type tables) so a first-time reader hits a working, copyable example within the first scroll. The dense reference tables can follow for readers who need them.

Add a brief one-line clarification in the trigger tables for what 'n/a' means in the 'Default statistic' column. Currently a reader doesn't know if n/a means 'not applicable' (the trigger computes a ratio rather than aggregating raw values) or 'not configurable'. A parenthetical like '(ratio computed directly — statistic not applicable)' would remove ambiguity.

The 'Other resources' subsection under 'Defining alarms on resources' is very thin — two sentences that only name resource types and their triggers without an example. Consider either adding a short SQS queue alarm example (it's a common use case) or folding the content into the preceding subsections with a note.

Consider adding a brief pricing callout (even just the ~$0.10/alarm/month figure already present in the FAQ) near the top of the page as a lightweight <Info> or inline sentence. It's in the FAQ but many readers don't reach FAQs — surfacing it earlier would help with cost-aware decision-making.

The 'Alarm description' and 'History tracking' subsections are short enough to merge into the 'Notification targets' section rather than standing as separate H3s. This would tighten the page and prevent the heading-to-content ratio from looking disproportionate.

FAQ entry 'Can I alarm on custom CloudWatch metrics?' is currently dependent on the ungrounded application-load-balancer-custom claim. Once that claim is narrowed, this answer should be updated to match the corrected prose — otherwise the FAQ will contradict the main body.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Trigger-type tables are accurate and well-organized — default comparison operators are correctly differentiated for 'less than' triggers (free-storage, free-memory) vs 'greater than' for the rest, matching the source JSDoc.

Evaluation window section is the strongest part of the page: the math is correct (period × evaluationPeriods = total window), the concrete example is realistic, and the Tip callout gives actionable starting values for production vs storage alarms.

CLI vs Console distinction is clean throughout — `stacktape debug:alarms` is correctly named and linked, and the Console section is scoped to what the Console source actually shows (view, create, delete global rules), without inventing navigation paths.
Shell commands correctly use one-per-block format with prose introductions — no copy-buffer trap.
FAQ covers all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS-service use-case, and choosing/comparison) with 9 substantive Q&A pairs. None are padded or redundant.

'When to use / When NOT to use' sections are opinionated and concrete — they name specific metrics and specific alternatives (logs for debugging, issues for application errors, budgets for cost), not circular justifications.
The `<Info>` callout about `INSUFFICIENT_DATA` state is a high-value production gotcha that developers reliably hit right after deployment — well placed.

Code examples use valid class names (`LambdaFunction`, `StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging`, `RelationalDatabase`, `RdsEnginePostgres`) and configure realistic, non-trivial scenarios (combined CPU + free-storage with tuned evaluation window).
problems: The `application-load-balancer-custom` trigger interface is referenced in the union type in the source but its definition (`ApplicationLoadBalancerCustomTrigger`) is not in the provided source files. The page asserts specific property names (`threshold` and `metric`) and claims '50+ CloudWatch ALB metrics' — both are unverifiable from the provided source and could be wrong.

Notification target property names (`conversationId`, `accessToken`, `sender`, `recipient`, `webhookUrl`, `url`, `secret`) are not visible in any provided source file. Only the union type names (`SlackIntegration`, `MsTeamsIntegration`, etc.) are present. If any of these property names are wrong, the reader configures a broken alarm and gets no error until runtime.

Web service alarms section: `StpWebService` is absent from the `StpAlarmEnabledResource` union in the source (`StpLambdaFunction | StpRelationalDatabase | StpHttpApiGateway | StpApplicationLoadBalancer | StpSqsQueue`). The claim that web services accept ALB or HTTP API Gateway alarm triggers via their `alarms` property is not grounded in the provided source.

The `disabledGlobalAlarms` property and its documented behavior (takes alarm names as configured in Console, does not affect inline alarms) are not present in `alarms.d.ts` or any other provided source file. The property might exist on individual resource types, but that is unverifiable from what was provided.

'Alarm description' and 'History tracking' are H3s nested under the 'Notification targets' H2, but `description` and `includeInHistory` are properties of `AlarmDefinitionBase` — they apply to all alarms, not specifically to notifications. This nesting implies a relationship the types don't have.
mandatoryFixes: Narrow or qualify the `application-load-balancer-custom` trigger section. The interface definition is not in the provided source, so the property names (`threshold`, `metric`) and the '50+ metrics' claim are unsupported. Either remove the specific property names and replace with 'see API reference for available properties', or add a caveat that these reflect the underlying CloudWatch configuration. Do not assert specific interface properties that aren't visible in the source.

Remove or qualify the notification target property names table (`conversationId`, `accessToken`, `sender`, `recipient`, `webhookUrl`, `url`, `secret`). These are not in the provided source. Replace with: 'See [alert channels](/observability/alert-channels) for the required properties for each integration type.' Do not document properties you cannot verify.

Remove the 'Web service alarms' subsection or reframe it explicitly as inference. `StpWebService` is not in `StpAlarmEnabledResource`. If web services do support alarms, the authoritative location is the web-service type definition, not the alarm types file. Claiming this without source grounding could send the reader on a dead-end configuration path.

Move 'Alarm description' and 'History tracking' out from under the 'Notification targets' H2. Both properties (`description`, `includeInHistory`) belong to `AlarmDefinitionBase` and apply to any alarm regardless of notification targets. Create a separate H2 section (e.g., 'Alarm metadata') or fold them into the intro of 'Defining alarms on resources'.
optionalImprovements: The `disabledGlobalAlarms` code example and prose are useful — if you can verify the property name from the LambdaFunction or resource type definitions, keep it. If it can't be grounded, remove the example and replace with a one-sentence note that individual resources can opt out of global alarms (link to the specific resource page where it's documented).

Add a note that `period` must be a multiple of 60 — this is stated in the evaluation table's description column but easy to miss. A one-line callout or bold note near the evaluation window intro would save the reader a deploy-time validation error.

The database alarm example shows `instanceClass: 'db.t4g.micro'` inside `primaryInstance` without explaining that this is the RDS instance size selector. One sentence noting that `instanceClass` controls the RDS instance tier (and that `db.t4g.micro` is the smallest/cheapest option) would follow the property-value anchor rule and help readers adapting the example.

The FAQ answer to 'Can I alarm on custom CloudWatch metrics?' correctly limits the claim to the `application-load-balancer-custom` trigger. However, if that trigger's properties are unverified (see mandatory fix above), this FAQ answer also needs tightening — remove the specific metric examples (`ActiveConnectionCount`, `TargetResponseTime`, etc.) if the interface definition isn't confirmed.

Consider adding a brief note in the 'Viewing alarm states' → Console subsection that the Console's alarm rules page is organization-scoped (not per-stack), which explains why you need the CLI for per-stage alarm state. The source confirms this distinction but the page doesn't make it explicit enough for a reader who just deployed their first stack.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and close to the 40-75 word target.
Trigger coverage is well organized with tables, which helps extraction and quick comparison.
Stacktape config examples use <CodeBlock intellisense> with TypeScript tabs and avoid forbidden YAML/plain-object config patterns.
CLI and Console alarm viewing are separated instead of blurred, and CLI snippets are one command per block.
problems: The page includes unsupported cost claims in the FAQ, including a specific CloudWatch alarm price and 'negligible' cost language, despite the page instructions saying cost is separate and the provided sources containing no pricing data.

Several Stacktape-specific behavior claims are not grounded in the provided sources: global rules being automatically attached at deploy time, inline alarms being merged with global rules, and Stacktape routing alarm changes through EventBridge to a service Lambda.

The draft documents `disabledGlobalAlarms`, but that property is not present in the provided `alarms.d.ts` source. If the property exists in resource source files, those files are not in the source list for this review.

The draft says web services accept HTTP API Gateway or ALB alarm triggers, but the provided `StpAlarmEnabledResource` union lists Lambda functions, relational databases, HTTP API Gateways, Application Load Balancers, and SQS queues only.
Console workflow wording goes beyond the provided Console source in places, especially claims about creating global rules and using them to enforce monitoring standards across all projects.
The FAQ is useful but includes unsupported implementation detail and pricing content, which weakens source-grounding for an answer-engine audience.
Some advice is too prescriptive without source support, such as production stages needing at least one error-rate alarm per public-facing resource and one storage/CPU alarm per database.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or replace the CloudWatch pricing FAQ. If cost context is needed, link to `/managing-costs/*` without quoting prices because no pricing source is provided and the observability instructions say cost belongs elsewhere.

Remove or source-ground the claims that inline alarms are merged with global alarm rules, global rules automatically attach during deployment, and alarm state changes route through EventBridge to a Stacktape service Lambda.
Remove `disabledGlobalAlarms` from the page unless the relevant resource type source is added and proves the property. If retained, explicitly ground it in the correct source file.

Correct the supported-resource wording to match the provided `StpAlarmEnabledResource` source: Lambda functions, relational databases, HTTP API Gateways, Application Load Balancers, and SQS queues. Do not claim web-service alarm support from these sources.

Narrow Console claims to what `AlarmsRulesPage.tsx` proves: the Console alarm rules page lists rules, shows name/created/projects/stages/trigger columns, opens a create modal, shows details, and supports deletion. Avoid unsourced deploy-time or organization-enforcement behavior.
optionalImprovements: Add a compact table distinguishing config-time inline alarms from Console alarm rules, with a 'source proves' level of certainty in the wording.
Add one or two sentences after each example explaining why the shown thresholds are example values, not defaults or recommended universal settings.
Trim unsupported operational recommendations or phrase them as examples rather than requirements.

Make the 'Viewing alarm states' H2 first paragraph more self-contained by saying 'Stacktape exposes alarm state through the `stacktape debug:alarms` CLI command...' before splitting CLI and Console details.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly aligned with the alarm trigger type definitions, evaluation defaults, notification target variants, and Console alarm-rules table. The main blockers are unsupported web-service alarm support, unsupported alarm-as-Lambda-trigger behavior, and several Console/CLI/runtime claims that go beyond the provided sources.

issues.statement: “A web service accepts both HTTP API Gateway and Application Load Balancer alarm triggers… Web services accept the trigger types of their underlying networking resource.” Also in FAQ: “Stacktape supports inline alarms on … web services.”
issues.reason: The alarm-enabled resource union in the provided type source does not include web services. It lists Lambda functions, relational databases, HTTP API Gateways, Application Load Balancers, and SQS queues only. The draft would lead readers to configure `alarms` on a web service, which is not grounded by the provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove web services from the supported inline alarm resources. Say that alarms are supported on Lambda functions, relational databases, HTTP API Gateways, Application Load Balancers, and SQS queues. If a web service exposes an underlying gateway/load balancer elsewhere in config, do not claim web-service-level `alarms` support unless a source file proves it.

issues.statement: “Inline alarms are merged with any matching global alarm rules created in the Console.” “Both sets of alarms are active simultaneously.” “At deploy time, Stacktape merges global rules with inline alarms.”
issues.reason: The source defines inline alarm shapes and global alarm-rule fields (`forServices`, `forStages`) but does not describe deploy-time merge behavior or simultaneous activation semantics. issues.suggestedFix: Use narrower wording: inline alarms are configured on supported resources, while Console alarm rules can be scoped by project and stage. Avoid claiming merge timing or simultaneous activation unless a deployment/runtime source is provided.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Comprehensive trigger-type tables with threshold property names, units, default statistics, and comparison operators — all tightly matched to the source types in alarms.d.ts.
Three realistic, copy-paste-ready TypeScript config examples (Lambda alarm, database with evaluation tuning, Lambda with Slack notifications and a p95 duration alarm) that cover the main use cases.
Clear CLI vs Console separation throughout: debug:alarms for per-stack alarm state, Console for global rule management — directly meeting the section requirement.

Evaluation window section explains the period/evaluationPeriods/breachedPeriods interplay with a concrete worked example (25-minute window, 3-of-5 breach) and a practical <Tip> starting configuration for production.

FAQ covers all three required categories: Stacktape-specific questions (disabling global alarms, inline vs global), AWS service questions (CloudWatch alarm cost, testing alarms), and comparison questions (alarms vs issues).
The info callout distinguishing LessThanThreshold defaults for free-storage and free-memory triggers is a source-grounded clarification that prevents a common misconfiguration.
disabledGlobalAlarms feature documented with a concrete, realistic use case (long-running scheduled Lambda tripping a duration alarm) that makes the decision clear.
problems: UNGROUNDED CLAIM: The `application-load-balancer-custom` trigger section describes `metric` and `threshold` properties in detail and claims '52 CloudWatch ALB metrics' — but the `ApplicationLoadBalancerCustomTrigger` interface is NOT defined in the provided alarms.d.ts. None of these specifics are grounded in any provided source file. The same ungrounded detail is repeated verbatim in the FAQ answer for 'What does the application-load-balancer-custom trigger support?'
INVENTED NUMBER: The '52 CloudWatch ALB metrics' count is a specific number not found in any provided source. It reads as authoritative but is unverifiable and will drift as AWS adds metrics.

POTENTIALLY UNGROUNDED RESOURCE: The Supported Resources table lists 'Web service' with an `alarms` property, but the source's `StpAlarmEnabledResource` union covers only LambdaFunction, RelationalDatabase, HttpApiGateway, ApplicationLoadBalancer, and SqsQueue — no WebService. Whether web services expose a separate `alarms` property that delegates to their underlying networking resource is not confirmed by the provided source.

UNKNOWN MDX COMPONENT: `<ConsoleScreenshot>` is not in the documented MDX component palette (Info, Tip, Warning, ApiReference, ReferenceableParams, CodeBlock). If the component is not registered in the docs build, the 'Global alarm rules' section will silently render nothing or throw a build error.
mandatoryFixes: Remove the ungrounded `application-load-balancer-custom` trigger detail. The `ApplicationLoadBalancerCustomTrigger` interface does not appear in the provided alarms.d.ts, so the `metric` property name, `threshold` property name, the '52 metrics' count, and the list of example metric names (ActiveConnectionCount, TargetResponseTime, etc.) are all unsupported Stacktape-specific claims. Replace the paragraph with: 'The `application-load-balancer-custom` trigger lets you alarm on additional ALB CloudWatch metrics beyond the built-in error-rate and unhealthy-targets triggers. See the API reference for the supported properties.' Remove the same ungrounded content from the FAQ answer about the custom trigger.

Remove the specific '52 CloudWatch ALB metrics' count everywhere it appears. The source does not enumerate these metrics and the number is a specific claim that will become stale. Replace with non-numeric phrasing such as 'additional ALB CloudWatch metrics'.

Either ground the Web service row in the Supported Resources table by identifying a source file that shows WebService has its own `alarms` property, or remove the row entirely. The authoritative `StpAlarmEnabledResource` union in alarms.d.ts covers Lambda, RelationalDatabase, HttpApiGateway, ApplicationLoadBalancer, and SqsQueue — WebService is absent. If the row stays, the Info callout should clarify this is indirect (alarms attach to the underlying ALB or API Gateway resource, not the WebService itself), and the prose should not imply WebService is a first-class alarm-enabled resource.
optionalImprovements: Verify that `<ConsoleScreenshot>` is a valid registered MDX component in the docs build. If not, replace it with a prose description of the alarm rules page and a link, or convert to a standard `<Info>` callout. Leaving an unregistered component will silently break the section.

The database alarm code example uses `instanceClass: 'db.t4g.micro'` without explaining what the property controls or what alternatives exist. One sentence noting that `instanceClass` sets the database server size and linking to the relational database page would prevent the value from reading as a required magic string.

The ALB custom trigger table row lists 'Default statistic: avg' — but since the `ApplicationLoadBalancerCustomTrigger` interface is not in the provided source, this default cannot be verified. After narrowing the custom trigger description (per mandatory fix), either remove this table row or mark the statistic as 'see API reference'.

The Global alarm rules section explains `forServices` and `forStages` as Console-side properties but doesn't connect them to the same-named fields on the `AlarmDefinition` type. A brief parenthetical noting the Console terminology maps to the type fields would reduce confusion for readers who jump between the Console and their config.

The evaluation window Tip gives two good starting configurations but doesn't name the core trade-off: shorter periods catch issues faster but produce more false positives; longer periods or higher breachedPeriods reduce noise but delay detection. One sentence framing the trade-off before the specific recommendations would help readers calibrate to their own tolerance.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and within the target length.
